narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there amid the challenges many face in higher education. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from past students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 75.8% chance that you might not pass, which is why your application was rejected—schools rely on this to ensure strong outcomes for everyone. Your profile includes being in the lower third of your school's grading decile after Year 1 and even lower after Year 3, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, an LSAT score of 29.30, middle family income, and plans to attend full-time. The biggest factors pushing the prediction toward bar failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which stood out as notably lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass, followed by your Year 1 decile, also below average and reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Your LSAT score, while not top-tier compared to the usual 36.70 average for successful students, actually helped pull the prediction slightly toward passing by showing some potential. Your undergraduate GPA edged a bit toward failure risk since it's under the typical 3.21 for passers, your full-time status offered a small positive nudge as most successful students share that path, and family income had minimal sway. Overall, your grades in later school years were the most influential red flags when stacked against peers who passed the bar, painting a picture riskier than the typical admitted student. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential—many from similar backgrounds overcome these stats with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated this. Reapply after addressing these areas, perhaps with essays highlighting your unique resilience and story. You've got this—law needs voices like yours, and with strategic steps, you can shift the odds in your favor. We're rooting for you.
